feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: By Sebastian Felix Schwemer and Peter Alexander Earls Davis

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We welcome the Commission’s initiative to address digital fairness through targeted legislative action, following last year’s Digital Fairness Check of the consumer law. The proposed Digital Fairness Act (DFA) initiative represents a timely opportunity to enhance consumer protection in the digital environment.

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: At the same time, we encourage careful consideration of the scope, terminology, and coherence of the initiative to ensure that it achieves its objectives effectively and complements existing EU legislation. We offer the following observations and recommendations:

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Clarify Scope and Initiative Title to Avoid Overreach

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The initiative’s title, Digital Fairness Act, may lead to interpretive breadth that could dilute the initiative’s consumer protection focus. The notion of fairness (and its morphological derivatives) is inherently context-dependent and has been used across multiple legal and policy domains, from data protection and algorithmic accountability to market regulation, each with distinct normative and legal implications.

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Without clear boundaries, the DFA may attract expectations beyond its intended scope, such as data protection fairness, algorithmic fairness, and market fairness. During the AI Act’s legislative process, for example, the European Parliament proposed including the principles from the Commission’s 2020 Guidelines for Trustworthy AI, such as fairness, but this proposal was ultimately not adopted in the final Regulation.

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: To ensure greater clarity and coherence, we recommend renaming the initiative to Digital Consumer Fairness Act (or similar). This would explicitly anchor the proposal within the consumer acquis and signal its targeted focus. Should the Commission intend to address broader fairness dimensions that go beyond consumer law, this should be made explicit and supported by a dedicated impact assessment.

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Ensure Coherence Across the Digital Regulatory Acquis

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: In addition to the EU consumer law acquis, the DFA must be situated within the broader digital regulatory landscape, which includes the GDPR, DSA, DMA, ePrivacy Directive, AI Act, and others. These instruments already address many of the practices identified in the call for evidence and targeted by the DFA, e.g., dark patterns (DSA), profiling (GDPR) or influencer marketing (UCPD, DSA).

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Without careful delineation, the DFA risks duplicating existing provisions and exacerbating legal fragmentation (“without prejudice to”). These concerns are consistent with the Commission’s own findings in the recent call for evidence on the Digital Omnibus package and Digital Fitness Check (DG CONNECT), which highlighted that multiple horizontal and sector-specific digital rules have led to complex implementation, fragmented application and inconsistent enforcement.

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: In the context of the DFA, we recommend that the forthcoming legislative proposal prioritises horizontal coherence, simplification, and clear allocation of regulatory responsibilities. This includes clarifying which practices are addressed under which instruments and ensuring that new rules complement, rather than overlap or conflict with existing obligations.

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Sebastian Schwemer (Professor of Law and Technology, BI Norwegian Business School)

section_path: Submission on the Call for Evidence – Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Peter Davis (Postdoctoral Researcher, Faculty of Law, University of Copenhagen)
